title: Fibromyalgia
oneLiner: Chronic widespread pain with fatigue, sleep disturbance, and cognitive fog, typically in women aged 20–55; central sensitisation makes it real, not psychogenic.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 34-year-old woman presents with “pain all over” for 18 months — neck, shoulders, low back, both knees. She says, “I wake up more tired than when I went to bed,” and feels “like a fog” at work. She has seen rheumatology, orthopaedics, and neurology; all scans and bloods were normal. On exam, she winces when you press over the medial knee and the occiput, but there is no joint swelling or tenderness. Her PHQ-9 score is 14 (moderate depression). She has been told “it’s all in your head” and is frustrated.

**Dissect the vignette:**

| Pattern clue | What it tells you |
|--------------|-------------------|
| Woman, 30s–40s | Peak demographic; female:male ~ 7–9:1 |
| “All over” pain >3 months | Required for diagnosis – widespread (axial + left/right + above/below waist) |
| Non-restorative sleep | Key differentiating symptom – not just fatigue, but “unrefreshing” sleep |
| “Fibro fog” (cognitive complaints) | Central sensitisation affects concentration; helps distinguish from RA/SLE where cognition is spared unless steroid-induced |
| Multiple negative specialist workups | High utilisation of healthcare; rule-out diseases must be exhausted |
| Tender points (not just tender joints) | Lack of synovitis or tenosynovitis; pain is mechanical pressure hyperalgesia, not inflammatory |
| Moderate depression | Comorbid; can be both cause (central sensitisation) and consequence of chronic pain |
| Frustration / “gaslighting” | Common; patient may have seen >3 specialists; stigma is a barrier to therapeutic alliance |

**Atypical presentations:**

1. **Juvenile fibromyalgia** – adolescent girls with school absenteeism, headaches, and abdominal pain. Often missed as “growing pains” or anxiety. Look for sleep disturbance and allodynia on exam.
2. **Male fibromyalgia** – presents with regional pain (e.g. chronic low back or neck pain) and less reporting of fatigue. Tender point counts may be lower. More likely to be dismissed as malingering.
3. **Concomitant autoimmune disease** – a patient with well-controlled RA or SLE develops diffuse pain disproportionate to joint swelling. Tenderness is present at fibromyalgia sites, not over inflamed joints. This is “secondary fibromyalgia.” Mistaking flares for fibro leads to unnecessary immunosuppression.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
Fibromyalgia is a condition where the central nervous system amplifies pain signals (central sensitisation). There is no tissue damage – the brain and spinal cord become hypersensitive, making normal sensory input feel painful. It also disrupts sleep, mood, energy, and focus.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Prevalence:** ~2–4% of general population, 10× more common in women. A GP will see several per month; in rheumatology clinics, 10–15% of new referrals.
- **Diagnosis is one of inclusion + exclusion:** No biomarker exists. Over-investigating wastes resources; under-diagnosing perpetuates patient distress and disability.
- **Common pitfalls:**
  - Attributing fatigue and pain to depression alone (ignores central sensitisation as a separate driver)
  - Prescribing opioids or NSAIDs (poor efficacy, high addiction and GI risk)
  - Failing to recognise fibromyalgia in a patient with a known autoimmune disease (leads to unnecessary steroids/immunosuppressants)
  - Telling the patient “it’s all in your head” (destroys trust; the pathology is real, in the brain).

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
Fibromyalgia is not a peripheral joint or muscle disease – it is a disorder of **central pain processing**. The story:

**Normal pain pathway:**  
Noxious stimulus → peripheral nociceptor → spinal cord (dorsal horn) → thalamus → sensory cortex. Pain is proportional to stimulus.

**In fibromyalgia:**  
The volume knob is turned up. The dorsal horn becomes hyperexcitable (central sensitisation). Normal pressure, temperature, or even thoughts become painful.

**Key mechanisms:**

| Mechanism | What it does | Clinical sign |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|
| **Glutamate excess** | Excitatory neurotransmitter in spinal cord; prolongs and amplifies pain signals | Widespread hyperalgesia – pain with palpation of muscles/tendons; tender points |
| **Substance P elevation** | Found in CSF of fibromyalgia patients at 2–3× normal; facilitates pain transmission | Allodynia – pain from normally non-painful stimuli (e.g. blood pressure cuff) |
| **Dopamine & serotonin dysregulation** | Decreased descending inhibition pathway from brainstem to spinal cord | Fatigue, poor sleep, mood disturbance, fibro fog |
| **HPA axis dysfunction** | Chronic stress → altered cortisol rhythm; reduced growth hormone (TH1/TH2 shift; poor muscle repair) | Non-restorative sleep (stage IV sleep disrupted); morning stiffness without joint swelling |
| **Small fibre neuropathy** (controversial but emerging) | Reduced intraepidermal nerve fibre density in some patients | Burning pain in hands/feet; may mimic diabetic neuropathy |

**How pattern recognition links to pathophys:**

- **“Widespread” pain** → Central sensitisation has no anatomical barrier; the whole body is affected.
- **Axial pain (neck, low back)** → Tender points cluster in trapezius, supraspinatus, gluteal areas – these are high-pressure zones where central sensitisation is most easily triggered.
- **Fatigue** → HPA axis plus neurotransmitter depletion; also disrupted sleep architecture eliminates restorative stage NREM.
- **Cognitive fog** → Prefrontal cortex suffers same neurotransmitter imbalance; tasks requiring sustained attention become effortful.
- **Multiple tender points** (≥11 of 18) → Each point corresponds to a region where afferent input is amplified; pressure reproduces the patient’s typical pain.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
Most fibromyalgia is **idiopathic**, but several triggers are known. Each cause leaves a “stem clue.”

| Category | Triggers / causes | Stem clue |
|----------|-------------------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic (most common, ~70%)** | No identifiable cause | Gradual onset over months; no clear inciting event |
| **Infectious** | Epstein-Barr virus, Lyme disease, COVID-19, viral hepatitis | “Pain and fatigue started after glandular fever” or “after a flu-like illness” |
| **Traumatic** | Motor vehicle accident, fall, physical assault | “Ever since the car crash, I’ve had widespread pain” |
| **Psychological / stress** | Major life event, chronic stress, childhood trauma (ACE score) | “My pain started after my divorce” or “I’ve always been a worrier” – look for comorbid anxiety/PTSD |
| **Genetic** | Family history (first-degree relative risk ~8×) | Mother or sibling with fibromyalgia; twin concordance ~50% |
| **Endocrine / metabolic** | Hypothyroidism, iron deficiency, vitamin D deficiency | Often secondary – treat underlying condition first (may resolve fibro symptoms) |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic (no trigger). However, in clinical stems, watch for a **preceding infection** – especially post-viral (EBV, COVID-19) – as a classic exam vignette: “previously fit 28-year-old woman with prolonged fatigue and diffuse pain 6 weeks after mononucleosis.”

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnosis is clinical.** There is no confirmatory test. The role of investigations is to *rule out* mimics.

**Diagnostic criteria (ACR 2016):**

| Component | Threshold |
|-----------|-----------|
| **WPI (Widespread Pain Index)** | Score 0–19; list of 19 body regions where patient had pain in the last week. **Required: ≥7** (if also SSS ≥5) OR **≥4–6** (if also SSS ≥9) |
| **SSS (Symptom Severity Scale)** | Fatigue, waking unrefreshed, cognitive symptoms each scored 0–3 (0 = no problem, 3 = severe) + somatic symptom count. **Required: ≥5** (if WPI ≥7) OR **≥9** (if WPI 4–6) |
| **Duration** | Symptoms present at **similar level for ≥3 months** |
| **No other disorder** | Cannot be explained by another illness |

**Must rule out:**

| Test | Why order it | Interpretation |
|------|--------------|---------------|
| **ESR / CRP** | Screen for inflammatory arthritis/polymyalgia | **Normal** in fibromyalgia. If elevated, suspect RA, SLE, PMR, or vasculitis |
| **CBC + ferritin** | Anaemia, iron deficiency (fatigue mimic) | Low ferritin without anaemia can cause myalgia/fatigue; treat with iron before diagnosing fibro |
| **TSH** | Hypothyroidism (presenting with myalgia, fatigue, weight gain) | TSH >4.0 mIU/L suggests hypothyroidism; treat before diagnosing fibromyalgia |
| **Vitamin D** | Vitamin D deficiency causes widespread pain and myopathy | Level <20 ng/mL may be causing symptoms; replete before final diagnosis |
| **ANA, RF, anti-CCP** | Autoimmune connective tissue disease | Positive in SLE/RA. *But* up to 15% of fibromyalgia patients have low-titre ANA (false positive) – do not diagnose lupus on that alone |
| **CPK** | Inflammatory myopathy / statin myopathy | Elevated in myositis; normal in fibromyalgia |
| **Sleep study (polysomnography)** | Only if suspected sleep apnoea or restless legs | Alpha-delta sleep pattern (intrusion of alpha waves into NREM) is characteristic but not diagnostic; treat underlying sleep disorder first |

**Pattern-recognition: What confirms / rules out?**

- *Single finding that rules in:* **No lab test.** Diagnosis is made when typical history + exam + normal labs.
- *Single finding that rules out:* **ESR >50 or CRP >10 mg/L** (think inflammatory disease) **or CPK elevated** (think myositis).
- *Classic pattern:* Young woman with widespread pain, fatigue, sleep issues, **normal labs** – this is fibromyalgia until proven otherwise, but require a 3-month duration and no alternative explanation.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first encounter – not an emergency, but first hour of the consultation):**

| Action | Why |
|--------|-----|
| **Validate the diagnosis with empathy** | “Fibromyalgia is a real condition – it’s not in your head, it’s in your nervous system. You are not alone.” | Responds to “gaslighting” history; builds trust for treatment |
| **Give explanation + handout** | Explain central sensitisation using a pain volume knob analogy | Improves understanding; reduces health anxiety |
| **Address catastrophising** | Use brief cognitive reframing: “Pain does not mean damage. You can exercise safely.” | Prevents avoidant behaviour and disability spiral |
| **Initiate simple sleep hygiene** | “Go to bed and wake the same time daily; no screens 1 h before bed; avoid caffeine after 2 pm.” | Poor sleep worsens central sensitisation |
| **Avoid prescribing opioids or NSAIDs at this visit** | They have no evidence for fibromyalgia; opioids cause dependence and hyperalgesia | Prevents future harm |

**Ward (if admitted for other reasons – fibromyalgia patient with acute illness):**

| Action | Why |
|--------|-----|
| **Continue home meds (e.g. amitriptyline)** | Abrupt withdrawal worsens pain and sleep |
| **Encourage gentle mobilisation** | Prolonged bed rest increases deconditioning and pain |
| **Avoid patient labelling** | Do not write “drug-seeking” or “functional overlay” in notes without context |
| **Treat comorbid depression/anxiety** | If PHQ-9 ≥15 or GAD-7 ≥10, start SSRI (e.g. sertraline 50 mg PO daily, titrate to 100–150 mg) | Improves both mood and fibromyalgia symptoms |

**Long-term (core management – evidence-based):**

**Non-pharmacological (first-line – must be offered before drugs):**
- **Graded aerobic exercise:** Start 5–10 min walking daily, increase by 1–2 min/week, target 30 min moderate exercise 5×/week. *Reason:* decreases central sensitisation; increases endorphins.
- **Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT):** Reduces pain catastrophising, improves sleep, enhances coping. *Reason:* rewires maladaptive neural pathways.
- **Sleep hygiene + stimulus control:** As above; consider sleep restriction if insomnia prominent.

**Pharmacological (second-line, after failing 3 months of non-pharm):**

| Drug | Dose | Route | Duration | Key tips |
|------|------|-------|----------|----------|
| **Amitriptyline** | 10–50 mg | PO | Nocte | Start 10 mg, titrate weekly by 10–25 mg; anticholinergic side-effects (dry mouth, constipation); avoid in elderly >75 (fall risk) |
| **Duloxetine** | 30–60 mg daily | PO | Continuous | SNRI; 60 mg max; helps pain + mood; avoid with MAOIs; monitor for serotonin syndrome |
| **Pregabalin** | 150–300 mg daily (in 2 divided doses) | PO | Continuous | Start 75 mg BD, titrate to 150 mg BD; risk of dizziness, somnolence, weight gain; withdrawal symptoms on abrupt stop |
| **Gabapentin** (alternative) | 300–1200 mg TID | PO | Continuous | Less weight gain but more sedation; need dose adjustment in renal impairment |

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**

| Pitfall | Why to avoid |
|---------|--------------|
| **Prescribe opioid analgesics** | Evidence shows no benefit at 3 months; high risk of dependency, OIH (opioid-induced hyperalgesia), and constipation |
| **Long-term NSAIDs** | No anti-inflammatory target; GI and renal toxicity without benefit |
| **Oral corticosteroids** | No effect on central sensitisation; causes osteoporosis, weight gain, hyperglycaemia |
| **Trigger point injections with steroids** | Unproven; may cause local tissue damage; short-term placebo only |
| **Blindly follow tender point count** | ACR 2016 criteria deemphasise tender points; focus on symptoms |
| **Dismissing new joint swelling** | Fibromyalgia patients can develop RA/SLE too – if a joint is swollen, do not assume it's fibro |

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Fibromyalgia | Rheumatoid Arthritis | Polymyalgia Rheumatica | Myofascial Pain Syndrome |
|---------|-------------|----------------------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Pain** | Widespread, bilateral, symmetric, “aching,” duration >3 months | Symmetric small joints (MCP, PIP, wrists, ankles); morning stiffness >30 min | Proximal – shoulders, neck, hips; stiffness >1 hour; often bilateral | Regional, localised to a single muscle group (e.g., trapezius); trigger points with referred pain |
| **Exam** | Tender points (≥11/18); no synovitis; no swelling | Synovitis: swollen, tender joints; rheumatoid nodules in seropositive | No synovitis; pain on active shoulder/hip abduction; limited range | Palpable taut band in muscle; “twitch” response; pain referral pattern |
| **ECG** | Normal | Normal (pericarditis possible if rheumatoid vasculitis) | Normal | Normal |
| **Labs** | ESR/CRP normal; ANA may be low-titre; RF/CCP negative | ESR/CRP elevated; RF/anti-CCP positive; anaemia of chronic disease | ESR >40–100 mm/h; CRP elevated; normal RF/CCP | All normal |
| **Imaging** | Normal X-rays/MRI | Erosions, joint space narrowing on X-ray; synovitis on MRI/US | Normal X-ray; ± bursitis on MRI | Normal imaging; US may show myofascial trigger points if experienced |
| **Response to steroids** | No significant improvement | Dramatic improvement (prednisone 10–20 mg) | Dramatic improvement (prednisone 15–20 mg) | No improvement (local trigger point injections may help) |
| **Key differentiating clue** | **Multiple tender points + non-restorative sleep + normal labs** | **Swollen MCP/PIP + RF/CCP + erosions** | **Age >50, ESR >40, dramatic response to prednisolone** | **Regional pain, taut band, “jump sign”**

**Summary for pattern recognition:**
- *Fibromyalgia vs. RA:* If no swollen joints and normal CRP, it’s not RA.
- *Fibromyalgia vs. PMR:* Check age and ESR. PMR in a 34-year-old is very rare.
- *Fibromyalgia vs. myofascial:* Myofascial is focal; fibromyalgia is diffuse.

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Early / missed diagnosis leads to:**

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Chronic disability** | Patient stops working, becomes housebound, deconditioned | Multiple “off work” notes; muscle atrophy from disuse; weight gain | Refer to multidisciplinary pain programme; graded exercise therapy; occupational therapy |
| **Severe depression / suicidal ideation** | Hopelessness, anhedonia, sleep disturbance worsened, weight loss | PHQ-9 ≥20; passive or active suicidal thoughts; previous attempts | **Urgent referral to psychiatry / crisis team** – do not manage alone; remove access to means |
| **Opioid dependence** | Iatrogenic – patient was prescribed opioids early, now escalating doses, requesting early refills, “lost prescriptions” | Urine drug screen abnormal; opioid withdrawal symptoms between doses | Refer to addiction service; switch to non-opioid alternatives; consider buprenorphine/naloxone for detox if dependent |
| **Secondary fibromyalgia – missed underlying disease** | Patient labelled fibromyalgia but actually has RA, SLE, or hypothyroidism | Worsening joint swelling, rash, abnormal labs on repeat check | Revisits differential; recheck ESR, CRP, ANA, TSH; if positive, treat underlying disease |
| **Sleep apnoea unmasked** | Extreme daytime sleepiness, snoring, witnessed apnoeas, morning headache | BMI >30, STOP-BANG ≥3; oxygen desaturation on oximetry | Arrange polysomnogram; CPAP may resolve fibralgia if apnoea is the driver |

**Late-presentation patterns (the “what happens if untreated” stem):**

“A 45-year-old woman with a 10-year history of widespread pain, treated only with paracetamol and ibuprofen, presents to ED after a fall. She has lost 20 kg unintentionally, is withdrawn, and confides she ‘can’t go on.’”  
→ This is **depressive crisis with suicidality** due to unmanaged fibromyalgia. Immediate psychiatry consult; start antidepressant + pain management referral.

“A 55-year-old man with fibromyalgia (diagnosed 5 years ago) now presents with acute low back pain, fever, and urinary retention. CRP 200, MRI shows spondylodiscitis.”  
→ Missed new diagnosis because “it’s just his fibro.” Beware of **cognitive anchoring**.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **FIBRO** (symptom cluster for diagnosis):
   - **F** – Fatigue (persistent, >3 months)
   - **I** – Insomnia / non-restorative sleep
   - **B** – Body pain (widespread, axial + all 4 quadrants)
   - **R** – Restless sleep / waking unrefreshed
   - **O** – Other symptoms: cognitive fog, headache, IBS, dysmenorrhea, anxiety

2. **SLEEP** (non-pharm first steps):
   - **S** – Schedule (fixed bedtime/rise time)
   - **L** – Lights off (dark room, no screens)
   - **E** – Exercise (gentle, graded)
   - **E** – Education (CBT, pain reprocessing)
   - **P** – Pain diary (to identify flares vs. triggers)

3. **3 D's** to avoid in management:
   - **Don’t** give opioids
   - **Don’t** give steroids
   - **Don’t** dismiss

**High-yield pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **Pain “everywhere” + normal exam + normal labs = fibromyalgia until proven otherwise.** But always check TSH, vitamin D, ferritin first.
2. **Fibromyalgia and depression are not the same thing.** Treating depression alone improves mood but may not improve pain. Both need simultaneous management.
3. **Tender points are not necessary for diagnosis** (ACR 2016 dropped the exact 11/18 threshold). But if you find them on exam, they are suggestive.
4. **Secondary fibromyalgia occurs in 20–30% of patients with RA, SLE, and axial SpA.** Do not escalate immunosuppression for pain that is out of proportion to joint inflammation.
5. **The single best predictor of outcome is the patient’s belief about their pain.** If they think it’s a progressive, damaging disease, they’ll worsen. If they understand central sensitisation, they’ll engage in exercise and CBT.
6. **Graded exercise is not optional** – it is the most evidence-based treatment. Start low (5 min/day) and go slow.
7. **Avoid the words “psychosomatic” or “functional” unless you have a therapeutic relationship** – these terms can be stigmatising. Use “central sensitisation” or “amplified pain syndrome” instead.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**

| Mistake | Correct approach |
|---------|------------------|
| Ordering repeat imaging or ANA “just to be safe” | Frequent false positives; causes more anxiety and referrals. Stick to clinical criteria after negative basic screen |
| Stopping amitriptyline abruptly because “it didn’t work at 25 mg” | Requires 6–8 weeks at therapeutic dose (50–75 mg); taper slowly if discontinuing |
| Prescribing pregabalin without renal function check | Accumulates in renal impairment (dizziness, confusion); reduce dose if eGFR <30 |
| Assuming fibromyalgia is “low acuity” and can be seen next week in clinic | Patients often have comorbid severe depression -> suicide risk. Do not leave without screening PHQ-9. If suicidal, escalate |

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
Fibromyalgia itself is not an acute emergency, but its complications are. Escalate as below:

| Situation | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **Suicidal ideation with plan** | PHQ-9 item 9 ≥1 (“thoughts of harming yourself”); or verbalised plan | **Immediate**: Psychiatry emergency assessment; consider Mental Health Act; one-to-one supervision; remove belongings |
| **Opioid withdrawal / overdose** | Opioid withdrawal: COWS >12; overdose: respiratory rate <10, GCS <8 | **Crash call** for overdose (naloxone 0.4–2 mg IV q2min). Withdrawal: ward-based detox with clonidine 0.1–0.2 mg PO PRN |
| **Severe depression with weight loss, anorexia** | BMI <16, food refusal, low albumin | **Add psychiatry + dietitian; consider NG feeding** |
| **New neurological signs** | Weakness, sensory level, bladder/bowel dysfunction | **Urgent MRI spine** – transverse myelitis or cord compression may mimic fibromyalgia but is

## Fibromyalgia
**One-liner:** Chronic widespread pain with fatigue, sleep disturbance, and cognitive fog, typically in women aged 20–55; central sensitisation makes it real, not psychogenic.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**  
A 34-year-old woman presents with “pain all over” for 18 months — neck, shoulders, low back, both knees. She says, “I wake up more tired than when I went to bed,” and feels “like a fog” at work. She has seen rheumatology, orthopaedics, and neurology; all scans and bloods were normal. On exam, she winces when you press over the medial knee and the occiput, but there is no joint swelling or tenderness. Her PHQ-9 score is 14 (moderate depression). She has been told “it’s all in your head” and is frustrated.

**Dissect the vignette:**

| Pattern clue | What it tells you |
|--------------|-------------------|
| Woman, 30s–40s | Peak demographic; female:male ~ 7–9:1 |
| “All over” pain >3 months | Required for diagnosis – widespread (axial + left/right + above/below waist) |
| Non-restorative sleep | Key differentiating symptom – not just fatigue, but “unrefreshing” sleep |
| “Fibro fog” (cognitive complaints) | Central sensitisation affects concentration; helps distinguish from RA/SLE where cognition is spared unless steroid-induced |
| Multiple negative specialist workups | High utilisation of healthcare; rule-out diseases must be exhausted |
| Tender points (not just tender joints) | Lack of synovitis or tenosynovitis; pain is mechanical pressure hyperalgesia, not inflammatory |
| Moderate depression | Comorbid; can be both cause (central sensitisation) and consequence of chronic pain |
| Frustration / “gaslighting” | Common; patient may have seen >3 specialists; stigma is a barrier to therapeutic alliance |

**Atypical presentations:**

1. **Juvenile fibromyalgia** – adolescent girls with school absenteeism, headaches, and abdominal pain. Often missed as “growing pains” or anxiety. Look for sleep disturbance and allodynia on exam.
2. **Male fibromyalgia** – presents with regional pain (e.g. chronic low back or neck pain) and less reporting of fatigue. Tender point counts may be lower. More likely to be dismissed as malingering.
3. **Concomitant autoimmune disease** – a patient with well-controlled RA or SLE develops diffuse pain disproportionate to joint swelling. Tenderness is present at fibromyalgia sites, not over inflamed joints. This is “secondary fibromyalgia.” Mistaking flares for fibro leads to unnecessary immunosuppression.

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
Fibromyalgia is a condition where the central nervous system amplifies pain signals (central sensitisation). There is no tissue damage – the brain and spinal cord become hypersensitive, making normal sensory input feel painful. It also disrupts sleep, mood, energy, and focus.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Prevalence:** ~2–4% of general population, 10× more common in women. A GP will see several per month; in rheumatology clinics, 10–15% of new referrals.
- **Diagnosis is one of inclusion + exclusion:** No biomarker exists. Over-investigating wastes resources; under-diagnosing perpetuates patient distress and disability.
- **Common pitfalls:**
  - Attributing fatigue and pain to depression alone (ignores central sensitisation as a separate driver)
  - Prescribing opioids or NSAIDs (poor efficacy, high addiction and GI risk)
  - Failing to recognise fibromyalgia in a patient with a known autoimmune disease (leads to unnecessary steroids/immunosuppressants)
  - Telling the patient “it’s all in your head” (destroys trust; the pathology is real, in the brain).

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

Fibromyalgia is not a peripheral joint or muscle disease – it is a disorder of **central pain processing**. The story:

**Normal pain pathway:**  
Noxious stimulus → peripheral nociceptor → spinal cord (dorsal horn) → thalamus → sensory cortex. Pain is proportional to stimulus.

**In fibromyalgia:**  
The volume knob is turned up. The dorsal horn becomes hyperexcitable (central sensitisation). Normal pressure, temperature, or even thoughts become painful.

**Key mechanisms:**

| Mechanism | What it does | Clinical sign |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|
| **Glutamate excess** | Excitatory neurotransmitter in spinal cord; prolongs and amplifies pain signals | Widespread hyperalgesia – pain with palpation of muscles/tendons; tender points |
| **Substance P elevation** | Found in CSF of fibromyalgia patients at 2–3× normal; facilitates pain transmission | Allodynia – pain from normally non-painful stimuli (e.g. blood pressure cuff) |
| **Dopamine & serotonin dysregulation** | Decreased descending inhibition pathway from brainstem to spinal cord | Fatigue, poor sleep, mood disturbance, fibro fog |
| **HPA axis dysfunction** | Chronic stress → altered cortisol rhythm; reduced growth hormone (TH1/TH2 shift; poor muscle repair) | Non-restorative sleep (stage IV sleep disrupted); morning stiffness without joint swelling |
| **Small fibre neuropathy** (controversial but emerging) | Reduced intraepidermal nerve fibre density in some patients | Burning pain in hands/feet; may mimic diabetic neuropathy |

**How pattern recognition links to pathophys:**

- **“Widespread” pain** → Central sensitisation has no anatomical barrier; the whole body is affected.
- **Axial pain (neck, low back)** → Tender points cluster in trapezius, supraspinatus, gluteal areas – these are high-pressure zones where central sensitisation is most easily triggered.
- **Fatigue** → HPA axis plus neurotransmitter depletion; also disrupted sleep architecture eliminates restorative stage NREM.
- **Cognitive fog** → Prefrontal cortex suffers same neurotransmitter imbalance; tasks requiring sustained attention become effortful.
- **Multiple tender points** (≥11 of 18) → Each point corresponds to a region where afferent input is amplified; pressure reproduces the patient’s typical pain.

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

Most fibromyalgia is **idiopathic**, but several triggers are known. Each cause leaves a “stem clue.”

| Category | Triggers / causes | Stem clue |
|----------|-------------------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic (most common, ~70%)** | No identifiable cause | Gradual onset over months; no clear inciting event |
| **Infectious** | Epstein-Barr virus, Lyme disease, COVID-19, viral hepatitis | “Pain and fatigue started after glandular fever” or “after a flu-like illness” |
| **Traumatic** | Motor vehicle accident, fall, physical assault | “Ever since the car crash, I’ve had widespread pain” |
| **Psychological / stress** | Major life event, chronic stress, childhood trauma (ACE score) | “My pain started after my divorce” or “I’ve always been a worrier” – look for comorbid anxiety/PTSD |
| **Genetic** | Family history (first-degree relative risk ~8×) | Mother or sibling with fibromyalgia; twin concordance ~50% |
| **Endocrine / metabolic** | Hypothyroidism, iron deficiency, vitamin D deficiency | Often secondary – treat underlying condition first (may resolve fibro symptoms) |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic (no trigger). However, in clinical stems, watch for a **preceding infection** – especially post-viral (EBV, COVID-19) – as a classic exam vignette: “previously fit 28-year-old woman with prolonged fatigue and diffuse pain 6 weeks after mononucleosis.”

---

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnosis is clinical.** There is no confirmatory test. The role of investigations is to *rule out* mimics.

**Diagnostic criteria (ACR 2016):**

| Component | Threshold |
|-----------|-----------|
| **WPI (Widespread Pain Index)** | Score 0–19; list of 19 body regions where patient had pain in the last week. **Required: ≥7** (if also SSS ≥5) OR **≥4–6** (if also SSS ≥9) |
| **SSS (Symptom Severity Scale)** | Fatigue, waking unrefreshed, cognitive symptoms each scored 0–3 (0 = no problem, 3 = severe) + somatic symptom count. **Required: ≥5** (if WPI ≥7) OR **≥9** (if WPI 4–6) |
| **Duration** | Symptoms present at **similar level for ≥3 months** |
| **No other disorder** | Cannot be explained by another illness |

**Must rule out:**

| Test | Why order it | Interpretation |
|------|--------------|---------------|
| **ESR / CRP** | Screen for inflammatory arthritis/polymyalgia | **Normal** in fibromyalgia. If elevated, suspect RA, SLE, PMR, or vasculitis |
| **CBC + ferritin** | Anaemia, iron deficiency (fatigue mimic) | Low ferritin without anaemia can cause myalgia/fatigue; treat with iron before diagnosing fibro |
| **TSH** | Hypothyroidism (presenting with myalgia, fatigue, weight gain) | TSH >4.0 mIU/L suggests hypothyroidism; treat before diagnosing fibromyalgia |
| **Vitamin D** | Vitamin D deficiency causes widespread pain and myopathy | Level <20 ng/mL may be causing symptoms; replete before final diagnosis |
| **ANA, RF, anti-CCP** | Autoimmune connective tissue disease | Positive in SLE/RA. *But* up to 15% of fibromyalgia patients have low-titre ANA (false positive) – do not diagnose lupus on that alone |
| **CPK** | Inflammatory myopathy / statin myopathy | Elevated in myositis; normal in fibromyalgia |
| **Sleep study (polysomnography)** | Only if suspected sleep apnoea or restless legs | Alpha-delta sleep pattern (intrusion of alpha waves into NREM) is characteristic but not diagnostic; treat underlying sleep disorder first |

**Pattern-recognition: What confirms / rules out?**

- *Single finding that rules in:* **No lab test.** Diagnosis is made when typical history + exam + normal labs.
- *Single finding that rules out:* **ESR >50 or CRP >10 mg/L** (think inflammatory disease) **or CPK elevated** (think myositis).
- *Classic pattern:* Young woman with widespread pain, fatigue, sleep issues, **normal labs** – this is fibromyalgia until proven otherwise, but require a 3-month duration and no alternative explanation.

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first encounter – not an emergency, but first hour of the consultation):**

| Action | Why |
|--------|-----|
| **Validate the diagnosis with empathy** | “Fibromyalgia is a real condition – it’s not in your head, it’s in your nervous system. You are not alone.” | Responds to “gaslighting” history; builds trust for treatment |
| **Give explanation + handout** | Explain central sensitisation using a pain volume knob analogy | Improves understanding; reduces health anxiety |
| **Address catastrophising** | Use brief cognitive reframing: “Pain does not mean damage. You can exercise safely.” | Prevents avoidant behaviour and disability spiral |
| **Initiate simple sleep hygiene** | “Go to bed and wake the same time daily; no screens 1 h before bed; avoid caffeine after 2 pm.” | Poor sleep worsens central sensitisation |
| **Avoid prescribing opioids or NSAIDs at this visit** | They have no evidence for fibromyalgia; opioids cause dependence and hyperalgesia | Prevents future harm |

**Ward (if admitted for other reasons – fibromyalgia patient with acute illness):**

| Action | Why |
|--------|-----|
| **Continue home meds (e.g. amitriptyline)** | Abrupt withdrawal worsens pain and sleep |
| **Encourage gentle mobilisation** | Prolonged bed rest increases deconditioning and pain |
| **Avoid patient labelling** | Do not write “drug-seeking” or “functional overlay” in notes without context |
| **Treat comorbid depression/anxiety** | If PHQ-9 ≥15 or GAD-7 ≥10, start SSRI (e.g. sertraline 50 mg PO daily, titrate to 100–150 mg) | Improves both mood and fibromyalgia symptoms |

**Long-term (core management – evidence-based):**

**Non-pharmacological (first-line – must be offered before drugs):**
- **Graded aerobic exercise:** Start 5–10 min walking daily, increase by 1–2 min/week, target 30 min moderate exercise 5×/week. *Reason:* decreases central sensitisation; increases endorphins.
- **Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT):** Reduces pain catastrophising, improves sleep, enhances coping. *Reason:* rewires maladaptive neural pathways.
- **Sleep hygiene + stimulus control:** As above; consider sleep restriction if insomnia prominent.

**Pharmacological (second-line, after failing 3 months of non-pharm):**

| Drug | Dose | Route | Duration | Key tips |
|------|------|-------|----------|----------|
| **Amitriptyline** | 10–50 mg | PO | Nocte | Start 10 mg, titrate weekly by 10–25 mg; anticholinergic side-effects (dry mouth, constipation); avoid in elderly >75 (fall risk) |
| **Duloxetine** | 30–60 mg daily | PO | Continuous | SNRI; 60 mg max; helps pain + mood; avoid with MAOIs; monitor for serotonin syndrome |
| **Pregabalin** | 150–300 mg daily (in 2 divided doses) | PO | Continuous | Start 75 mg BD, titrate to 150 mg BD; risk of dizziness, somnolence, weight gain; withdrawal symptoms on abrupt stop |
| **Gabapentin** (alternative) | 300–1200 mg TID | PO | Continuous | Less weight gain but more sedation; need dose adjustment in renal impairment |

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**

| Pitfall | Why to avoid |
|---------|--------------|
| **Prescribe opioid analgesics** | Evidence shows no benefit at 3 months; high risk of dependency, OIH (opioid-induced hyperalgesia), and constipation |
| **Long-term NSAIDs** | No anti-inflammatory target; GI and renal toxicity without benefit |
| **Oral corticosteroids** | No effect on central sensitisation; causes osteoporosis, weight gain, hyperglycaemia |
| **Trigger point injections with steroids** | Unproven; may cause local tissue damage; short-term placebo only |
| **Blindly follow tender point count** | ACR 2016 criteria deemphasise tender points; focus on symptoms |
| **Dismissing new joint swelling** | Fibromyalgia patients can develop RA/SLE too – if a joint is swollen, do not assume it's fibro |

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Fibromyalgia | Rheumatoid Arthritis | Polymyalgia Rheumatica | Myofascial Pain Syndrome |
|---------|-------------|----------------------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Pain** | Widespread, bilateral, symmetric, “aching,” duration >3 months | Symmetric small joints (MCP, PIP, wrists, ankles); morning stiffness >30 min | Proximal – shoulders, neck, hips; stiffness >1 hour; often bilateral | Regional, localised to a single muscle group (e.g., trapezius); trigger points with referred pain |
| **Exam** | Tender points (≥11/18); no synovitis; no swelling | Synovitis: swollen, tender joints; rheumatoid nodules in seropositive | No synovitis; pain on active shoulder/hip abduction; limited range | Palpable taut band in muscle; “twitch” response; pain referral pattern |
| **ECG** | Normal | Normal (pericarditis possible if rheumatoid vasculitis) | Normal | Normal |
| **Labs** | ESR/CRP normal; ANA may be low-titre; RF/CCP negative | ESR/CRP elevated; RF/anti-CCP positive; anaemia of chronic disease | ESR >40–100 mm/h; CRP elevated; normal RF/CCP | All normal |
| **Imaging** | Normal X-rays/MRI | Erosions, joint space narrowing on X-ray; synovitis on MRI/US | Normal X-ray; ± bursitis on MRI | Normal imaging; US may show myofascial trigger points if experienced |
| **Response to steroids** | No significant improvement | Dramatic improvement (prednisone 10–20 mg) | Dramatic improvement (prednisone 15–20 mg) | No improvement (local trigger point injections may help) |
| **Key differentiating clue** | **Multiple tender points + non-restorative sleep + normal labs** | **Swollen MCP/PIP + RF/CCP + erosions** | **Age >50, ESR >40, dramatic response to prednisolone** | **Regional pain, taut band, “jump sign”**

**Summary for pattern recognition:**
- *Fibromyalgia vs. RA:* If no swollen joints and normal CRP, it’s not RA.
- *Fibromyalgia vs. PMR:* Check age and ESR. PMR in a 34-year-old is very rare.
- *Fibromyalgia vs. myofascial:* Myofascial is focal; fibromyalgia is diffuse.

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Early / missed diagnosis leads to:**

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Chronic disability** | Patient stops working, becomes housebound, deconditioned | Multiple “off work” notes; muscle atrophy from disuse; weight gain | Refer to multidisciplinary pain programme; graded exercise therapy; occupational therapy |
| **Severe depression / suicidal ideation** | Hopelessness, anhedonia, sleep disturbance worsened, weight loss | PHQ-9 ≥20; passive or active suicidal thoughts; previous attempts | **Urgent referral to psychiatry / crisis team** – do not manage alone; remove access to means |
| **Opioid dependence** | Iatrogenic – patient was prescribed opioids early, now escalating doses, requesting early refills, “lost prescriptions” | Urine drug screen abnormal; opioid withdrawal symptoms between doses | Refer to addiction service; switch to non-opioid alternatives; consider buprenorphine/naloxone for detox if dependent |
| **Secondary fibromyalgia – missed underlying disease** | Patient labelled fibromyalgia but actually has RA, SLE, or hypothyroidism | Worsening joint swelling, rash, abnormal labs on repeat check | Revisits differential; recheck ESR, CRP, ANA, TSH; if positive, treat underlying disease |
| **Sleep apnoea unmasked** | Extreme daytime sleepiness, snoring, witnessed apnoeas, morning headache | BMI >30, STOP-BANG ≥3; oxygen desaturation on oximetry | Arrange polysomnogram; CPAP may resolve fibralgia if apnoea is the driver |

**Late-presentation patterns (the “what happens if untreated” stem):**

“A 45-year-old woman with a 10-year history of widespread pain, treated only with paracetamol and ibuprofen, presents to ED after a fall. She has lost 20 kg unintentionally, is withdrawn, and confides she ‘can’t go on.’”  
→ This is **depressive crisis with suicidality** due to unmanaged fibromyalgia. Immediate psychiatry consult; start antidepressant + pain management referral.

“A 55-year-old man with fibromyalgia (diagnosed 5 years ago) now presents with acute low back pain, fever, and urinary retention. CRP 200, MRI shows spondylodiscitis.”  
→ Missed new diagnosis because “it’s just his fibro.” Beware of **cognitive anchoring**.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **FIBRO** (symptom cluster for diagnosis):
   - **F** – Fatigue (persistent, >3 months)
   - **I** – Insomnia / non-restorative sleep
   - **B** – Body pain (widespread, axial + all 4 quadrants)
   - **R** – Restless sleep / waking unrefreshed
   - **O** – Other symptoms: cognitive fog, headache, IBS, dysmenorrhea, anxiety

2. **SLEEP** (non-pharm first steps):
   - **S** – Schedule (fixed bedtime/rise time)
   - **L** – Lights off (dark room, no screens)
   - **E** – Exercise (gentle, graded)
   - **E** – Education (CBT, pain reprocessing)
   - **P** – Pain diary (to identify flares vs. triggers)

3. **3 D's** to avoid in management:
   - **Don’t** give opioids
   - **Don’t** give steroids
   - **Don’t** dismiss

**High-yield pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **Pain “everywhere” + normal exam + normal labs = fibromyalgia until proven otherwise.** But always check TSH, vitamin D, ferritin first.
2. **Fibromyalgia and depression are not the same thing.** Treating depression alone improves mood but may not improve pain. Both need simultaneous management.
3. **Tender points are not necessary for diagnosis** (ACR 2016 dropped the exact 11/18 threshold). But if you find them on exam, they are suggestive.
4. **Secondary fibromyalgia occurs in 20–30% of patients with RA, SLE, and axial SpA.** Do not escalate immunosuppression for pain that is out of proportion to joint inflammation.
5. **The single best predictor of outcome is the patient’s belief about their pain.** If they think it’s a progressive, damaging disease, they’ll worsen. If they understand central sensitisation, they’ll engage in exercise and CBT.
6. **Graded exercise is not optional** – it is the most evidence-based treatment. Start low (5 min/day) and go slow.
7. **Avoid the words “psychosomatic” or “functional” unless you have a therapeutic relationship** – these terms can be stigmatising. Use “central sensitisation” or “amplified pain syndrome” instead.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**

| Mistake | Correct approach |
|---------|------------------|
| Ordering repeat imaging or ANA “just to be safe” | Frequent false positives; causes more anxiety and referrals. Stick to clinical criteria after negative basic screen |
| Stopping amitriptyline abruptly because “it didn’t work at 25 mg” | Requires 6–8 weeks at therapeutic dose (50–75 mg); taper slowly if discontinuing |
| Prescribing pregabalin without renal function check | Accumulates in renal impairment (dizziness, confusion); reduce dose if eGFR <30 |
| Assuming fibromyalgia is “low acuity” and can be seen next week in clinic | Patients often have comorbid severe depression -> suicide risk. Do not leave without screening PHQ-9. If suicidal, escalate |

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

Fibromyalgia itself is not an acute emergency, but its complications are. Escalate as below:

| Situation | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **Suicidal ideation with plan** | PHQ-9 item 9 ≥1 (“thoughts of harming yourself”); or verbalised plan | **Immediate**: Psychiatry emergency assessment; consider Mental Health Act; one-to-one supervision; remove belongings |
| **Opioid withdrawal / overdose** | Opioid withdrawal: COWS >12; overdose: respiratory rate <10, GCS <8 | **Crash call** for overdose (naloxone 0.4–2 mg IV q2min). Withdrawal: ward-based detox with clonidine 0.1–0.2 mg PO PRN |
| **Severe depression with weight loss, anorexia** | BMI <16, food refusal, low albumin | **Add psychiatry + dietitian; consider NG feeding** |
| **New neurological signs** | Weakness, sensory level, bladder/bowel dysfunction | **Urgent MRI spine** – transverse myelitis or cord compression may mimic fibromyalgia but is
